perturbation.original_text: a. "Proprietary Information" means information including, but not limited to clients, pricing and information which is related to the business of Health Card from which Health Card (1) derives economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) is the subject of efforts by Health Card that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy, including without limitation (i) with respect to information which has been reduced to tangible form, marking such information clearly and conspicuously with a legend identifying its confidential or proprietary nature; (ii) with respect to any oral presentation or communication, denominating such information as confidential immediately before, during or after such oral presentation or communication; or (iii) otherwise treating such information as confidential. Assuming the criteria in clauses (1) and (2) above are met, Proprietary Information includes, but is not limited to technical and nontechnical information and data related to the formulas, patterns, designs, compilations, programs, inventions, methods, techniques, drawings, processes, finances, actual or potential customers and suppliers, research, development, existing and future products, and employees of Health Card.
perturbation.changed_text: a. "Proprietary Information" means information including, but not limited to clients, pricing and information which is related to the business of Health Card from which Health Card (1) derives economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) is the subject of efforts by Health Card that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy, excluding any measures to specifically mark such information clearly as confidential or proprietary. Assuming the criteria in clause (1) above is met, Proprietary Information includes, but is not limited to technical and nontechnical information and data related to the formulas, patterns, designs, compilations, programs, inventions, methods, techniques, drawings, processes, finances, actual or potential customers and suppliers, research, development, existing and future products, and employees of Health Card.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy including marking tangible information. By removing the necessity of clearly marking confidential information, this creates ambiguity and weakens the legal protection typically afforded to trade secrets under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that is subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Removing the specific requirement to mark tangible information as confidential undermines these reasonable efforts, making it more difficult to prove the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, including marking tangible information as confidential. The modified contract removes the specific requirement to mark tangible information, potentially undermining the 'reasonable efforts' needed to maintain trade secret protection. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that removing the requirement undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Although the scraped law snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation directly links the change to a weakening of trade secret protection under the UTSA, which is a legally recognized standard.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information also includes information which has been disclosed to Health Card by a third party, which Health Card is obligated to treat as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information also includes information which has been disclosed to Health Card by a third party, but Health Card's obligation to treat this as confidential is discretionary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the obligation from 'is obligated' to 'is discretionary' directly contradicts common law principles of confidential relationships and potentially violates NDAs that Health Card has with other parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: This alteration contradicts the duty of an agent (Health Card in this scenario) to protect the confidential information of its principal (the third party disclosing the information). Section 396 outlines the agent's continuing duty to protect confidential information received during the agency relationship.

perturbation.justification: The original text states Health Card 'is obligated' to treat third-party information as confidential, while the changed text makes this obligation 'discretionary.' This change contradicts the principle that agents must protect confidential information, as the discretionary clause weakens the protection that was originally in place.

perturbation.original_text: b. Proprietary Information does not include any information which (1) is already known to Ciufo at the time it is disclosed to Ciufo by Health Card, provided that such information has been rightfully received by Ciufo from a third party without restriction on disclosure and without breach of an obligation of confidentiality running directly or indirectly to Health Card; (2) is disclosed by Ciufo pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or is required to be disclosed by operation of law; provided, however, that Ciufo shall first have given written notice of such required disclosure to Health Card to allow Health Card to seek to protect the confidentiality of the information required to be disclosed; or (3) before being divulged by Ciufo (i) has become generally known to the public through no wrongful act of Ciufo (ii) has been rightfully received by Ciufo from a third party without restriction on disclosure and without breach of an obligation of confidentiality running directly or indirectly to Health Card; (iii) has been approved for release to the general public by written authorization of Health Card; (iv) has been independently developed by Ciufo without use, directly or indirectly, of the Proprietary Information; or (v) has been furnished to a third party by Health Card without restrictions on the third party's right to disclose the information.
perturbation.changed_text: b. Proprietary Information does not include any information which (1) is already known to Ciufo at the time it is disclosed to Ciufo by Health Card; (2) is disclosed by Ciufo pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or is required to be disclosed by operation of law, without any obligation to notify Health Card; or (3) before being divulged by Ciufo (i) has become generally known to the public through no wrongful act of Ciufo (ii) has been rightfully received by Ciufo from a third party (iii) has been approved for release to the general public by written authorization of Health Card; (iv) has been independently developed by Ciufo without use, directly or indirectly, of the Proprietary Information; or (v) has been furnished to a third party by Health Card without restrictions on the third party's right to disclose the information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for Ciufo to notify Health Card before disclosing information required by a governmental agency contradicts the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts, and the 'provided that' clause is removed from section (1) to ensure all previous knowledge is excluded.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. By eliminating the notification requirement, the contract allows Ciufo to potentially disclose sensitive information without giving Health Card an opportunity to protect its interests, arguably violating the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract eliminates the requirement for Ciufo to notify Health Card before disclosing information required by a governmental agency. The Law Explanation states that by removing this notification requirement, the contract allows Ciufo to potentially disclose sensitive information without giving Health Card an opportunity to protect its interests, arguably violating the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Thus, removing the notification requirement directly contradicts the obligation of good faith, as Health Card cannot enforce their contract rights if they are not even notified of the potential disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Ciufo acknowledges and agrees that, in the event of any breach or threatened breach of any provision of this agreement, Health Card may be without an adequate remedy at law and, accordingly, shall be entitled to enforce such provision by temporary or permanent injunctive or mandatory relief obtained in an action or proceeding instituted in any court of competent jurisdiction without the necessity of proving damages and without prejudice to any other rights or remedies which it may have at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Ciufo acknowledges and agrees that, in the event of any breach or threatened breach of any provision of this agreement, Health Card's sole remedy shall be limited to monetary damages not to exceed $1000, and Health Card waives any right to injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the remedy to only monetary damages and waiving injunctive relief could be seen as an unconscionable limitation of remedies, especially if proprietary information is critical to Health Card's business. This may violate principles of equity and fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the UCC deals with contractual modification or limitation of remedy. While it permits limitations, it states that where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. Limiting the remedy to $1000 could be deemed to fail of its essential purpose if the actual damages are much greater, or where injunctive relief is more appropriate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits Health Card's remedy to monetary damages not exceeding $1000 and waives injunctive relief, which contradicts the UCC as described in snippet 1. UCC § 2-719(2) states that "Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act." Limiting the remedy to a small amount might fail its essential purpose if actual damages are greater or injunctive relief is more appropriate, thus creating a legal contradiction. The perturbation explanation also supports this by noting it could be an unconscionable limitation.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information furnished by Health Card to Ciufo is considered loaned for use solely in connection with the Project, and shall be returned by Ciufo to Health Card upon request by Health Card. Ciufo shall certify that it has destroyed or returned all copies of the Proprietary Information in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information furnished by Health Card to Ciufo is considered gifted for use solely in connection with the Project, and Ciufo has no obligation to return or destroy the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'loaned' to 'gifted' removes the expectation of return and contradicts the fundamental purpose of a confidentiality agreement. The proprietary information is no longer protected because the contract now explicitly states there is no obligation to return or destroy the copies. This undermines state trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating the information is gifted and not to be returned, the contract loses the 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain secrecy element which is essential for information to qualify as a trade secret under UTSA. This could be used to show there was no intent for it to be kept secret.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text treats the proprietary information as 'loaned', implying an obligation to return it. The changed text states the information is 'gifted' and there is 'no obligation to return or destroy' it. This directly contradicts the intent to protect proprietary information, as the Law Explanation states this removes the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly state this, the change in the text fundamentally alters the nature of the agreement concerning proprietary information, negating efforts to maintain secrecy, and creating a legal contradiction by undermining trade secret protection.
